---
title: "Allrecipes Data Final Project"
subtitle: "INFO 526 - Summer 2026 - Final Project"
author: "Olivia Dodge"
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: images/watercolour_sys02_img34_teacup-ocean.jpg
  data-background-size: stretch
  data-background-opacity: "0.7"
  data-slide-number: none
format:
  revealjs:
    theme:  ['data/customtheming.scss']
  
editor: visual
execute:
  echo: false
---

```{r}
#| label: load-packages
#| include: false

# Load packages here
pacman::p_load(tidyverse,
               tidytuesdayR)

```

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

# Plot theme
ggplot2::theme_set(ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11))

# For better figure resolution
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  fig.retina = 3, 
  dpi = 300, 
  fig.width = 6, 
  fig.asp = 0.618 
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: load-data
#| include: false
# Load data here
tuesdata <- tidytuesdayR::tt_load('2025-09-16')
all_recipes <- tuesdata$all_recipes
cuisines <- tuesdata$cuisines

recipes_time <- all_recipes |>
  filter(!is.na(total_time), !is.na(avg_rating), total_time > 0) |>
  mutate(
    time_group = case_when(
      total_time < 30 ~ "Quick (<30 min)",
      total_time >= 30 & total_time <= 60 ~ "Moderate (30-60 min)",
      total_time > 60 ~ "Long (>60 min)"
    ),
    time_group = factor(time_group,
                        levels = c("Quick (<30 min)", "Moderate (30-60 min)", "Long (>60 min)"))
  )

top_cuisines <- cuisines |>
  count(country, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 3) |>
  pull(country)

cuisines_top <- cuisines |>
  filter(country %in% top_cuisines) |>
  filter(!is.na(protein), !is.na(fat), !is.na(carbs))

cuisines_long <- cuisines_top |>
  select(country, protein, fat, carbs) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(protein, fat, carbs),
               names_to = "nutrient",
               values_to = "grams")
```

## Motivation

- Recipe websites are full of options, but home cooks often choose recipes based on time available and cuisine preferences

- Two questions arise:

  - Does the time a recipe takes relate to how well users rate it?
  - Do different cuisines have meaningfully different nutritional profiles?

- Understanding these patterns can help both recipe platforms and home cooks make more informed choices

## The Data

- Two datasets from the TidyTuesday project (2025-09-16), sourced from an online recipe platform

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**`all_recipes`**

- 14,426 recipes
- Includes prep time, cook time, total time, nutrition, and user ratings
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**`cuisines`**

- 2,218 recipes
- Same variables, plus `country` of origin
:::
:::::

- Both datasets include nutritional content (calories, fat, carbs, protein) and popularity metrics (average rating, number of reviews)

## Research Questions

::: incremental
1.  **How do prep time, cook time, and total time relate to recipe ratings?**
    - Are quicker or longer recipes rated more favorably?
2.  **How do protein, fat, and carb content vary across cuisines?**
    - Focusing on the three most common cuisines: Brazilian, Canadian, and Filipino
:::

## Question 1: Time vs. Ratings {.smaller}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
ggplot(recipes_time, aes(x = total_time, y = avg_rating)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", color = "darkred", se = TRUE) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 300)) +
  labs(x = "Total Time (minutes)", y = "Average Rating") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
ggplot(recipes_time, aes(x = time_group, y = avg_rating, fill = time_group)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(x = "Time Group", y = "Average Rating") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none", axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))
```
:::
:::::

- Ratings stay roughly flat (\~4.5) regardless of total time - time is not a strong predictor of rating

## Question 2: Nutrients by Cuisine {.smaller}

```{r}
#| warning: false
ggplot(cuisines_long, aes(x = country, y = grams, fill = country)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.alpha = 0.3) +
  facet_wrap(~ nutrient, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(x = "Cuisine (Country)", y = "Grams") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none", axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))
```

- Filipino recipes show the highest average protein; Canadian recipes the highest average carbs

## Conclusions

- **Time does not strongly predict rating**: recipes are rated similarly whether quick, moderate, or long, suggesting users self-select recipes that match their expectations

- **Cuisine is associated with modest nutrient differences**: Filipino recipes trend higher in protein, Canadian recipes trend higher in carbs, variability within cuisines exists

- **Limitations**: small sample sizes per cuisine and natural self-selection in ratings limit how strongly these patterns can be generalized
